seoTitle: LEM Product Selection Guide | Current & Voltage Transducers | LiTong Electronics
seoDescription: Complete LEM product selection guide. Explore Hall effect current transducers, voltage transducers, industrial sensors, and automotive sensors. Authorized distributor with technical support.

faqs.question: How do I select the right LEM current transducer for my application?
faqs.answer: Selecting the right LEM current transducer involves considering several key factors: current range (choose 20-30% higher than maximum expected current), accuracy requirements (0.1-0.5% for closed-loop, 1-2% for open-loop), bandwidth needs (100-200kHz for closed-loop, 10-50kHz for open-loop), environmental conditions (temperature range, vibration, EMC), mounting constraints (panel, PCB, or busbar mount), and output type (voltage or current). For automotive applications, ensure AEC-Q100 qualification. Contact our FAE team for personalized selection guidance based on your specific requirements.
faqs.decisionGuide: If you need high precision (better than 1%), choose closed-loop sensors like CAS or HAIS series. For cost-sensitive applications where 1-2% accuracy is sufficient, open-loop sensors like HLSR series are ideal. For automotive EV applications, select AEC-Q100 qualified sensors from HO or HMSR series.

faqs.question: What is the difference between LEM open-loop and closed-loop current transducers?
faqs.answer: Open-loop Hall sensors measure magnetic field directly using a Hall element, offering simple structure, lower cost, and smaller size. They typically provide 1-2% accuracy with 10-50kHz bandwidth, suitable for general industrial applications. Closed-loop Hall sensors use a compensation coil and feedback mechanism to null the magnetic field, achieving 0.1-0.5% accuracy with 100-200kHz bandwidth. Closed-loop sensors have better linearity, lower temperature drift, and faster response but are larger, more expensive, and consume more power. The choice depends on your accuracy requirements, budget, and application constraints.
faqs.decisionGuide: Choose open-loop sensors (HLSR series) for cost-sensitive applications like HVAC, lighting control, and general motor monitoring. Choose closed-loop sensors (CAS, HAIS, HO series) for precision applications like servo drives, power analyzers, medical equipment, and EV battery management where accuracy is critical.

faqs.question: Can LEM sensors measure both DC and AC currents?
faqs.answer: Yes, LEM Hall effect current transducers can measure both DC and AC currents. The Hall effect principle responds to the magnetic field generated by current flow, regardless of whether it's DC or AC. For DC measurement, the sensor provides a stable output proportional to the DC current. For AC measurement, the sensor accurately tracks sinusoidal and non-sinusoidal waveforms. The bandwidth specification indicates the maximum AC frequency the sensor can accurately measure - typically 10-50kHz for open-loop and 100-200kHz for closed-loop sensors. Some LEM models can also measure complex waveforms including PWM signals common in motor drives and inverters.
faqs.decisionGuide: All LEM current transducers measure both DC and AC. For pure DC applications like battery monitoring, any LEM sensor will work. For AC applications, consider the bandwidth requirement - standard 50/60Hz power monitoring works with any sensor, while high-frequency PWM applications need higher bandwidth closed-loop sensors.

faqs.question: What isolation voltage do LEM transducers provide?
faqs.answer: LEM current transducers provide galvanic isolation between primary (measured) and secondary (output) circuits. Standard industrial models typically offer 2.5kV to 6kV isolation voltage (RMS), tested according to IEC 61010-1 standards. High-voltage models can provide up to 12kV isolation for specialized applications. The isolation ensures safety by preventing high voltages from reaching low-voltage control circuits and eliminates ground loop issues. All LEM sensors undergo 100% production testing for dielectric withstand voltage. For EV applications, 3kV to 5kV isolation is standard for high-voltage battery systems.
faqs.decisionGuide: For standard industrial applications (up to 600V systems), 2.5kV isolation is sufficient. For high-voltage EV battery systems (400V-800V), select sensors with 3kV or higher isolation. For grid-tied applications or medium-voltage systems, consider sensors with 5kV or higher isolation ratings.

faqs.question: How do I purchase LEM products from LiTong Electronics?
faqs.answer: As an authorized LEM distributor, LiTong Electronics offers multiple ways to purchase: 1) Contact our sales team directly via phone or email for quotations; 2) Submit an inquiry through our website contact form; 3) Send a purchase order to our sales department. We maintain stock of popular LEM models for fast delivery and can schedule deliveries for production programs. For large volume orders, we offer competitive pricing and flexible payment terms. Our minimum order quantity (MOQ) varies by product - standard industrial sensors typically have MOQ of 5-10 pieces, while automotive sensors may require 50-100 pieces. Contact us for specific pricing and availability.
faqs.decisionGuide: For prototype and small quantity needs (1-10 pieces), contact us for stock availability and pricing. For production volumes, we recommend establishing a scheduled order agreement for better pricing and supply security. For automotive programs, contact our automotive sales team early in the design phase for PPAP support and long-term supply agreements.

categories.name: Current Transducers
categories.description: Hall effect current transducers, covering open-loop and closed-loop technologies for precise current measurement in industrial and automotive applications
categories.longDescription: LEM current transducers utilize advanced Hall effect technology to provide isolated, non-contact current measurement solutions. The product line includes both open-loop and closed-loop architectures, catering to different accuracy and cost requirements. Open-loop Hall sensors offer cost-effective solutions for general industrial applications, while closed-loop Hall sensors deliver superior accuracy and bandwidth for demanding measurement tasks. All LEM current transducers feature galvanic isolation between primary and secondary circuits, ensuring safety and eliminating ground loop issues. With measurement ranges from milliamps to thousands of amps, these sensors serve diverse applications including motor drives, power supplies, renewable energy systems, and electric vehicles. As an authorized LEM distributor, LiTong Electronics provides comprehensive selection guidance and technical support to help you choose the optimal current transducer for your specific application requirements.
categories.selectionGuide: When selecting LEM current transducers, consider the following factors: 1) Current range - Choose a sensor with rated current 20-30% higher than your maximum expected current; 2) Accuracy requirements - Closed-loop sensors offer 0.1-0.5% accuracy, while open-loop sensors provide 1-2% accuracy; 3) Bandwidth needs - Closed-loop sensors typically offer 100-200kHz bandwidth vs 10-50kHz for open-loop; 4) Environmental conditions - Industrial applications use standard sensors (-40°C to +85°C), automotive requires AEC-Q100 certified parts (-40°C to +125°C); 5) Mounting constraints - Panel mount, PCB mount, or busbar mount options available; 6) Output type - Select voltage (0-5V, 0-10V) or current (4-20mA) output based on your system requirements.

faqs.question: What is the difference between open-loop and closed-loop Hall effect current transducers?
faqs.answer: Open-loop Hall sensors measure magnetic field directly using a Hall element, offering simple structure, lower cost, and smaller size. They typically provide 1-2% accuracy with 10-50kHz bandwidth, suitable for general industrial applications. Closed-loop Hall sensors use a compensation coil and feedback mechanism to null the magnetic field, achieving 0.1-0.5% accuracy with 100-200kHz bandwidth. Closed-loop sensors have better linearity, lower temperature drift, and faster response but are larger, more expensive, and consume more power. Choose open-loop for cost-sensitive applications and closed-loop when high precision is required.
faqs.decisionGuide: Choose open-loop sensors (HLSR series) for cost-sensitive applications like HVAC, lighting control, and general motor monitoring. Choose closed-loop sensors (CAS, HAIS series) for precision applications like servo drives, power analyzers, and medical equipment where accuracy is critical.

faqs.question: How do I select the appropriate current range for LEM transducers?
faqs.answer: When selecting current range, consider: 1) Normal operating current - typically 50-80% of rated current for optimal accuracy; 2) Peak current - ensure the sensor can handle transient overcurrents (most LEM sensors handle 2-3x rated current); 3) Safety margin - select rated current 20-30% above maximum expected current; 4) Measurement resolution - smaller range provides better resolution but less headroom; 5) Future expansion - consider potential system upgrades. For example, if your motor drive has 50A nominal current with 100A peak, a 100A rated sensor would be appropriate, providing good resolution while handling peak currents safely.
faqs.decisionGuide: For best accuracy, operate the sensor at 50-80% of rated current. Choose a sensor with rated current 1.3-1.5x your maximum continuous current. For applications with high inrush currents (motor starting, transformer energization), verify the sensor can handle the peak without saturation.

faqs.question: What isolation voltage do LEM current transducers provide?
faqs.answer: LEM current transducers provide galvanic isolation between primary (measured) and secondary (output) circuits. Standard industrial models typically offer 2.5kV to 6kV isolation voltage (RMS), tested according to IEC 61010-1 standards. High-voltage models can provide up to 12kV isolation for specialized applications. The isolation ensures safety by preventing high voltages from reaching low-voltage control circuits and eliminates ground loop issues. All LEM sensors undergo 100% production testing for dielectric withstand voltage. For specific isolation requirements in your application, consult the datasheet or contact our FAE team for guidance.
faqs.decisionGuide: For standard industrial applications (up to 600V systems), 2.5kV isolation is sufficient. For high-voltage EV battery systems (400V-800V), select sensors with 3kV or higher isolation. For grid-tied applications, consider sensors with 5kV or higher isolation ratings.

faqs.question: Can LEM current transducers measure DC and AC currents?
faqs.answer: Yes, LEM Hall effect current transducers can measure both DC and AC currents. The Hall effect principle responds to the magnetic field generated by current flow, regardless of whether it's DC or AC. For DC measurement, the sensor provides a stable output proportional to the DC current. For AC measurement, the sensor accurately tracks sinusoidal and non-sinusoidal waveforms. The bandwidth specification indicates the maximum AC frequency the sensor can accurately measure - typically 10-50kHz for open-loop and 100-200kHz for closed-loop sensors. Some LEM models can also measure complex waveforms including PWM signals common in motor drives and inverters.
faqs.decisionGuide: All LEM current transducers measure both DC and AC. For pure DC applications like battery monitoring, any LEM sensor will work. For AC applications, consider the bandwidth requirement - standard 50/60Hz power monitoring works with any sensor, while high-frequency PWM applications need higher bandwidth closed-loop sensors.

faqs.question: What are the typical applications for LEM current transducers?
faqs.answer: LEM current transducers are used in diverse applications: 1) Industrial drives - Motor current monitoring, overcurrent protection, vector control; 2) Power supplies - Input/output current monitoring, PFC circuits, DC-DC converters; 3) Renewable energy - PV inverter DC/AC current measurement, wind turbine power monitoring; 4) Electric vehicles - Battery management current sensing, motor controller feedback, charging station monitoring; 5) UPS systems - Battery charge/discharge monitoring, inverter current feedback; 6) Welding equipment - Primary and secondary current control; 7) Medical equipment - Isolated patient safety monitoring; 8) Test equipment - Precision current measurement and analysis.
faqs.decisionGuide: For motor drives and industrial automation, HLSR or CAS series are commonly used. For EV battery management and automotive, HO or HMSR series with AEC-Q100 qualification are required. For high-precision test equipment, CAS or HAIS series provide the best accuracy.

faqs.question: How do I install and wire LEM current transducers?
faqs.answer: LEM current transducers support multiple mounting options: 1) Panel mount - Secure with screws through mounting holes, pass conductor through aperture; 2) PCB mount - Solder pins directly to circuit board, conductor routed through sensor; 3) Busbar mount - Attach to busbar system for high-current applications. Wiring considerations: Connect supply voltage (+Vs, -Vs or GND) as specified; output connects to measurement circuit (voltage or current output); ensure proper polarity for DC measurements. For best performance: Keep primary conductor centered in aperture; avoid external magnetic fields; use twisted pair for output cables in noisy environments; maintain specified operating temperature range. Always refer to the specific product datasheet for detailed installation instructions.
faqs.decisionGuide: For new PCB designs, PCB-mount sensors like HLSR or HMSR offer the most compact solution. For retrofit applications or high currents, panel-mount sensors like CAS or HTFS are easier to install. For busbar applications, HAIS series provides robust mounting options.

products.name: HLSR 10-P Open-Loop Current Transducer
products.shortDescription: 10A rated open-loop Hall effect current transducer with voltage output, ideal for industrial automation and motor drives
descriptionParagraphs: The HLSR 10-P is a compact open-loop Hall effect current transducer designed for industrial applications requiring reliable current measurement up to 10A. It features a single supply voltage operation (3.3V or 5V), voltage output (0.5V to 4.5V centered at 2.5V), and excellent price-performance ratio.

Based on open-loop Hall effect technology, the HLSR series offers non-contact current measurement with galvanic isolation between primary and secondary circuits. The sensor provides 2.5kV isolation voltage and operates over a wide temperature range of -40°C to +105°C, making it suitable for demanding industrial environments.

The HLSR 10-P is particularly well-suited for applications such as motor drives, power supplies, lighting ballasts, and home appliances where cost-effective current monitoring is required. Its compact PCB-mount package allows for easy integration into space-constrained designs.

faeReview.author: Robert Liu
faeReview.content: The HLSR 10-P is an excellent choice for cost-sensitive applications requiring reliable current measurement. In our field experience, this sensor performs exceptionally well in motor drive applications where the 100kHz bandwidth adequately captures the fundamental current waveform. The 1% accuracy is sufficient for most protection and monitoring functions. Key advantages include the single supply operation which simplifies power supply design, and the ratiometric output that tracks supply voltage variations. One consideration is that being open-loop, it has higher temperature drift compared to closed-loop alternatives - approximately 0.5% per 10°C. For applications requiring higher precision, consider the CAS series closed-loop sensors. The HLSR 10-P is particularly popular in HVAC systems and small motor drives where cost is a primary concern.
highlight: Cost-effective solution for general industrial applications
Single supply operation simplifies power supply design
Ratiometric output tracks supply voltage variations

alternativeParts.reason: Higher current range (20A) for applications with larger current requirements
alternativeParts.comparison: HLSR 20-P offers 2x current range (20A vs 10A) with same 1% accuracy and identical package. Direct pin-compatible upgrade for higher current applications.

alternativeParts.reason: Closed-loop alternative with higher accuracy (0.5%) for precision applications
alternativeParts.comparison: CAS 6-NP provides superior 0.5% accuracy vs 1% for HLSR, with better temperature stability. Requires dual ±15V supply and has higher cost but delivers precision performance.

companionParts.relationship: Higher current range variant in same series
Higher current range variant in same series
Voltage transducer for combined current/voltage monitoring

faqs.question: What is the output voltage range of HLSR 10-P?
faqs.answer: The HLSR 10-P provides a ratiometric voltage output centered at Vcc/2 (2.5V for 5V supply, 1.65V for 3.3V supply). At zero current, output is Vcc/2. At +10A, output is approximately Vcc/2 + 2V (4.5V for 5V supply). At -10A, output is approximately Vcc/2 - 2V (0.5V for 5V supply). This ratiometric output ensures the sensor maintains accuracy even with supply voltage variations.
faqs.decisionGuide: The ratiometric output simplifies ADC interface design. If your microcontroller ADC uses the same 5V or 3.3V reference as the sensor supply, the digital reading will be independent of actual supply voltage variations.

faqs.question: Can HLSR 10-P measure currents higher than 10A?
faqs.answer: Yes, the HLSR 10-P can measure currents beyond its 10A rated value. The sensor has a linear measurement range of ±25A, though accuracy specifications are guaranteed only up to the rated 10A. Between 10A and 25A, the sensor continues to provide output but with reduced accuracy. Beyond ±25A, the output saturates. For continuous operation above 10A, consider the HLSR 20-P or HLSR 32-P models which offer higher rated current ranges.
faqs.decisionGuide: For occasional peak currents up to 25A, HLSR 10-P can be used. For continuous operation above 10A or frequent peaks, select HLSR 20-P (20A rated) or HLSR 32-P (32A rated) for better accuracy and linearity.

faqs.question: What PCB layout considerations apply to HLSR 10-P?
faqs.answer: For optimal HLSR 10-P performance: 1) Keep the current-carrying trace centered in the sensor aperture; 2) Maintain clearance between primary and secondary circuits per isolation requirements; 3) Place decoupling capacitors (100nF ceramic) close to supply pins; 4) Route output traces away from high-current paths to minimize interference; 5) Ensure adequate copper width for the measured current; 6) Consider thermal management for continuous high-current operation. The sensor footprint is compatible with standard PCB assembly processes.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use wide copper traces (minimum 2mm for 10A) for the current path through the sensor. Place the sensor away from high-frequency switching components to minimize EMI coupling. Add a 100nF ceramic capacitor between Vcc and GND as close to the sensor as possible.

products.name: CAS 6-NP Closed-Loop Current Transducer
products.shortDescription: 6A rated closed-loop Hall effect current transducer with high accuracy (0.5%) for precision measurement applications
descriptionParagraphs: The CAS 6-NP is a high-performance closed-loop Hall effect current transducer designed for applications requiring precise current measurement. With its 0.5% accuracy, fast 1μs response time, and 200kHz bandwidth, it delivers exceptional performance for demanding industrial and test applications.

Utilizing closed-loop Hall effect technology with magnetic flux compensation, the CAS series achieves superior linearity and temperature stability compared to open-loop alternatives. The sensor provides 3kV isolation voltage and operates from -40°C to +85°C, ensuring reliable operation in industrial environments.

The CAS 6-NP features a compact design with panel mounting capability and is available with current output (4-20mA or 0-20mA) or voltage output configurations. It is ideal for applications such as precision motor control, power analyzers, medical equipment, and test and measurement systems.
products.faeReview: The CAS 6-NP represents LEM's closed-loop technology at its finest. In precision applications like servo motor drives and power analyzers, the 0.5% accuracy and excellent linearity (0.1% typical) make a significant difference in control performance. The closed-loop architecture provides inherent compensation for temperature variations, maintaining accuracy across the full operating range. The sub-microsecond response time enables accurate capture of fast transient currents. One important consideration is the dual supply requirement (±12V to ±15V), which adds complexity compared to single-supply alternatives. However, for applications where accuracy is paramount, this trade-off is well justified. The CAS series is frequently specified in medical equipment and precision test instruments where measurement integrity is critical.

alternativeParts.reason: Higher current range (15A) for applications requiring larger current measurement
Lower cost open-loop alternative for less demanding accuracy requirements

companionParts.relationship: Higher current range variant in same series
Higher current range variant in same series
Voltage transducer for power measurement applications

faqs.question: Why choose closed-loop over open-loop for the CAS 6-NP?
faqs.answer: The closed-loop design of CAS 6-NP provides several advantages: 1) Superior accuracy - 0.5% vs 1-2% for open-loop; 2) Better linearity - 0.1% typical vs 1% for open-loop; 3) Lower temperature drift - compensated by feedback mechanism; 4) Faster response time - <1μs vs 3-5μs; 5) Higher bandwidth - 200kHz vs 100kHz; 6) No magnetic offset - flux is continuously nulled. These benefits make closed-loop sensors essential for precision applications like servo drives, power analyzers, and medical equipment where measurement accuracy directly impacts system performance.
faqs.decisionGuide: Choose CAS 6-NP when accuracy better than 1% is required, or when measuring dynamic currents with fast transients. For static or slowly varying currents where 1-2% accuracy is acceptable, consider HLSR series for cost savings.

faqs.question: What is the difference between CAS 6-NP and CASR 6-NP?
faqs.answer: The CAS and CASR series are similar closed-loop current transducers with slightly different specifications. CASR (R for Reduced) models typically offer a more compact form factor and may have slightly different electrical characteristics. The CAS 6-NP is the standard version with full performance specifications, while CASR variants may be optimized for specific applications or mounting configurations. Both series provide the same 0.5% accuracy and closed-loop performance. When selecting between them, consider mechanical fit, supply voltage requirements, and specific application needs. Our FAE team can help determine the best variant for your design.
faqs.decisionGuide: CAS series is the standard choice for most applications. CASR series should be considered when space is extremely limited or when specific mounting constraints require the reduced form factor. Both offer identical electrical performance.

faqs.question: How does the dual supply voltage work for CAS 6-NP?
faqs.answer: The CAS 6-NP requires dual supply voltages (±12V to ±15V) to power the internal compensation circuitry. The positive supply (+Vs) powers the output stage and Hall element, while the negative supply (-Vs) enables bidirectional current flow in the compensation coil. This dual supply configuration allows the sensor to accurately measure both positive and negative currents while maintaining fast response. When designing the power supply, ensure both rails are well-regulated and decoupled with capacitors close to the sensor pins. Some applications may require DC-DC converters to generate the negative supply from a single positive supply.
faqs.decisionGuide: Design your power supply to provide stable ±12V or ±15V rails. Use linear regulators for lowest noise, or switching regulators with adequate filtering. Place 100nF ceramic capacitors close to both supply pins for decoupling.

categories.name: Voltage Transducers
categories.description: Isolated voltage transducers providing high-precision voltage measurement solutions for industrial and power electronics applications
categories.longDescription: LEM voltage transducers offer isolated, high-precision voltage measurement using Hall effect technology combined with integrated primary resistors. These sensors provide galvanic isolation between the high-voltage primary circuit and low-voltage secondary electronics, ensuring safety and eliminating ground loop issues. The product range covers voltage measurement from tens of volts to thousands of volts, with various output options including instantaneous voltage, RMS voltage, and digital outputs. LEM voltage transducers feature excellent accuracy, wide bandwidth, and fast response times, making them ideal for power electronics applications such as inverters, converters, power supplies, and grid monitoring systems. All products undergo rigorous testing and comply with international safety standards. As an authorized LEM distributor, LiTong Electronics provides expert selection guidance to help you choose the right voltage transducer series for your specific measurement requirements.
categories.selectionGuide: Selecting the right LEM voltage transducer involves several considerations: 1) Voltage range - Choose rated voltage 20-30% above maximum expected voltage; 2) Measurement type - Instantaneous for waveform analysis, RMS for power monitoring; 3) Accuracy requirements - Standard models offer 0.5-1% accuracy, high-precision models available; 4) Response time - Critical for protection applications requiring fast detection; 5) Isolation requirements - Standard 2.5kV to high-voltage 12kV options; 6) Primary resistance - Higher resistance reduces power dissipation but affects frequency response; 7) Output type - Voltage output (0-5V, 0-10V) or current output (4-20mA) based on system compatibility. Contact our FAE team for application-specific recommendations.

faqs.question: How do LEM voltage transducers work?
faqs.answer: LEM voltage transducers use a combination of primary resistors and Hall effect current measurement. The measured voltage is applied across an integrated high-value resistor (primary resistor), creating a small current proportional to the voltage. This current flows through a Hall effect current transducer which provides isolated measurement. The Hall sensor output is processed to provide a voltage or current output proportional to the input voltage. This architecture provides inherent galvanic isolation between the high-voltage input and low-voltage output circuits. The primary resistor value determines the input impedance and power dissipation, with higher values offering lower power loss but potentially limiting bandwidth.
faqs.decisionGuide: For standard DC bus monitoring, LV series with integrated primary resistors are simplest. For very high voltages or custom requirements, consider adding external series resistors to share power dissipation and extend voltage range.

faqs.question: What is the difference between instantaneous and RMS voltage transducers?
faqs.answer: Instantaneous voltage transducers provide an output that follows the input voltage waveform in real-time, preserving amplitude and phase information. They are used for waveform analysis, synchronization, and control applications where the instantaneous voltage value matters. RMS (Root Mean Square) voltage transducers calculate the true RMS value of the input voltage over a defined time window, providing a DC output proportional to the AC voltage magnitude regardless of waveform shape. RMS sensors are used for power monitoring, metering, and protection applications where the effective voltage value is important. LEM offers both types - instantaneous models like LV series and RMS models like DVL series for different application needs.
faqs.decisionGuide: Choose instantaneous output (LV series) for control applications, waveform analysis, or when phase information is needed. Choose RMS output (DVL series) for metering, power monitoring, or when measuring distorted AC waveforms where true effective value matters.

faqs.question: How do I calculate power dissipation in voltage transducer primary resistors?
faqs.answer: Primary resistor power dissipation is calculated using P = V²/R, where V is the measured voltage and R is the primary resistance. For example, an LV 25-P measuring 500V with 12kΩ primary resistance dissipates P = 500²/12000 = 20.8W. This is significant and must be considered for thermal management. To reduce dissipation: 1) Select models with higher primary resistance; 2) Use external series resistors to share power; 3) Ensure adequate ventilation or heatsinking; 4) Consider that higher resistance may reduce bandwidth. LEM datasheets specify primary resistance values and maximum continuous voltage ratings. For high-voltage continuous operation, consult our FAE team for thermal design guidance.
faqs.decisionGuide: Calculate power dissipation at your maximum operating voltage. If exceeding 10W continuously, consider using external series resistors mounted on heatsinks to share the thermal load, or select a model with higher primary resistance.

faqs.question: Can LEM voltage transducers measure DC and AC voltages?
faqs.answer: Yes, LEM voltage transducers can measure both DC and AC voltages. The Hall effect principle responds to the current through the primary resistor, which is proportional to voltage regardless of DC or AC. For DC voltage measurement, the sensor provides a stable output proportional to the DC level. For AC voltage measurement, instantaneous output models accurately track the AC waveform, while RMS models provide a DC output proportional to the RMS value. The bandwidth specification indicates the maximum AC frequency that can be accurately measured. Most LEM voltage transducers have bandwidths from DC to several kHz, with some models extending to 100kHz or higher for high-frequency applications.
faqs.decisionGuide: All LEM voltage transducers measure DC. For AC applications, choose based on output type needed - LV series for instantaneous waveform tracking, DVL series for RMS measurement of AC or distorted waveforms.

faqs.question: What safety certifications do LEM voltage transducers have?
faqs.answer: LEM voltage transducers carry multiple safety certifications including: IEC 61010-1 for measurement, control, and laboratory equipment; UL 508 for industrial control equipment; EN 50178 for power electronic equipment; and UL 1577 for optocoupler isolation (applicable to isolation specifications). These certifications ensure the sensors meet rigorous safety standards for dielectric withstand, creepage distances, and clearance requirements. The isolation voltage ratings (2.5kV to 12kV depending on model) are verified through 100% production testing. For specific certification requirements in your application or region, consult the product datasheet or contact our technical support team.
faqs.decisionGuide: For general industrial applications in Europe, IEC 61010-1 certification is typically sufficient. For North American markets, UL 508 certification may be required. For medical applications, additional IEC 60601-1 certification may be necessary.

faqs.question: How do I connect external primary resistors for high-voltage measurement?
faqs.answer: For voltages exceeding the transducer's internal primary resistor rating, external resistors can be added in series: 1) Calculate required total resistance: R_total = V_max / I_primary (typically 10-50mA); 2) Subtract internal resistance to determine external resistor value; 3) Select resistors with adequate voltage rating and power dissipation capability; 4) Use multiple series resistors if single resistor voltage rating is insufficient; 5) Ensure proper creepage and clearance for high-voltage connections; 6) Consider temperature coefficient for accuracy requirements. The external resistors share voltage and power dissipation with the internal resistor. Always include safety margins and consult relevant safety standards for high-voltage design.
faqs.decisionGuide: For continuous operation above rated voltage, use external resistors to share power dissipation. Mount external resistors on heatsinks if total dissipation exceeds 5W. Use high-voltage resistors with adequate voltage rating (typically 500V-1000V per resistor).

products.name: LV 25-P Voltage Transducer
products.shortDescription: 500V rated isolated voltage transducer with 0.8% accuracy for industrial power electronics applications
descriptionParagraphs: The LV 25-P is a compact isolated voltage transducer designed for measuring DC, AC, and pulsed voltages up to 500V. It combines an integrated primary resistor with Hall effect isolation technology to provide safe, accurate voltage measurement in power electronics applications.

Featuring 0.8% accuracy, 2.5kV isolation voltage, and 10kHz bandwidth, the LV 25-P delivers reliable performance for inverter monitoring, power supply feedback, and industrial control systems. The sensor operates from a single 12-15V supply and provides instantaneous voltage output proportional to the measured voltage.

The LV 25-P is widely used in applications such as PV inverter DC bus monitoring, motor drive DC link measurement, UPS battery voltage sensing, and power supply output feedback. Its compact design and panel-mount capability simplify integration into existing equipment.
products.faeReview: The LV 25-P is a workhorse voltage transducer that has proven itself in countless industrial applications. Its 500V rating covers the majority of industrial DC bus applications, including 380V AC rectified systems. The 0.8% accuracy is excellent for most monitoring and control applications, though not quite precision grade. In our field experience, the LV 25-P performs reliably in harsh industrial environments with excellent long-term stability. The 10kHz bandwidth is adequate for fundamental voltage monitoring but may limit harmonic analysis applications. Key advantages include the integrated primary resistor (simplifying design), proven reliability, and cost-effectiveness. For higher voltage requirements, consider the LV 100 or LV 200 models. The LV 25-P is often paired with current transducers like the CAS series for complete power monitoring solutions.

alternativeParts.reason: Higher voltage rating (1000V) for medium-voltage applications
RMS output version for AC voltage monitoring applications

companionParts.relationship: Current transducer for combined voltage/current power monitoring
Higher voltage rating variant in same series
Current transducer for complete power measurement

faqs.question: What is the primary resistance of LV 25-P and why does it matter?
faqs.answer: The LV 25-P has an integrated primary resistance of 12kΩ. This resistance is crucial because: 1) It determines the input current at a given voltage (I = V/R), which affects power dissipation; 2) Along with the Hall sensor characteristics, it determines the overall transfer ratio; 3) It affects the input impedance seen by the measured circuit. At 500V rated voltage, the primary current is approximately 42mA, resulting in about 21W power dissipation in the internal resistor. This significant power dissipation requires thermal consideration in the design. The 12kΩ value provides a good balance between power dissipation and measurement performance for the 500V rating.
faqs.decisionGuide: The 12kΩ primary resistance results in significant power dissipation (21W at 500V). Ensure adequate ventilation or heatsinking in your design. For lower dissipation, consider adding external series resistors to share the power load.

faqs.question: Can LV 25-P measure voltages above 500V?
faqs.answer: The LV 25-P can measure voltages beyond its 500V rated value, but with important considerations: 1) The measurement range extends to ±1500V with reduced accuracy; 2) Continuous operation above 500V increases power dissipation and may cause overheating; 3) For sustained high-voltage operation, external series resistors should be added to share power dissipation; 4) The isolation rating (2.5kV) sets the absolute maximum safe voltage. For applications requiring continuous measurement above 500V, consider using the LV 100 (1000V rated) or adding appropriate external resistors. Always ensure thermal design accommodates the power dissipation at operating voltage.
faqs.decisionGuide: For occasional peaks up to 1000V, LV 25-P can be used. For continuous operation above 500V, use LV 100 (1000V rated) or add external series resistors to share power dissipation and prevent overheating.

faqs.question: How do I interpret the LV 25-P output signal?
faqs.answer: The LV 25-P provides a differential voltage output proportional to the measured primary voltage. The nominal conversion ratio is typically 2500:1000 (consult specific datasheet for exact ratio), meaning at 500V input, the output is approximately 200mV. The output is bipolar, representing both positive and negative input voltages. For a ±15V supply, the output typically centers around 0V with positive and negative excursions. To use the output: 1) Connect to a differential amplifier or ADC with appropriate input range; 2) Scale the output based on the conversion ratio to calculate input voltage; 3) Consider adding filtering if noise is present; 4) Ensure load impedance meets specifications (typically >10kΩ). The datasheet provides detailed transfer characteristics and connection diagrams.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use a differential amplifier or instrumentation amplifier to process the LV 25-P output. Scale the output to match your ADC input range. For noise-sensitive applications, add a low-pass filter with cutoff frequency above your signal bandwidth but below switching noise frequencies.

products.name: DVL 500 RMS Voltage Transducer
products.shortDescription: 500V rated true RMS voltage transducer with 0.5% accuracy for power monitoring and metering applications
descriptionParagraphs: The DVL 500 is a high-precision true RMS voltage transducer designed for AC voltage monitoring and power metering applications. Unlike instantaneous voltage transducers, the DVL 500 calculates and outputs the true RMS value of the input voltage, providing accurate measurement regardless of waveform distortion.

Featuring 0.5% accuracy, 2.5kV isolation, and DC voltage output proportional to RMS input, the DVL 500 is ideal for power quality monitoring, energy management systems, and industrial metering. The sensor handles input frequencies from DC to 100Hz and maintains accuracy even with distorted waveforms containing harmonics.

The DVL 500 is widely deployed in applications such as grid monitoring, generator set control, building energy management, and industrial power distribution systems where accurate voltage measurement is essential for billing, protection, or control purposes.
products.faeReview: The DVL 500 is the go-to solution when true RMS voltage measurement is required. Unlike simple average-responding sensors, it accurately measures distorted waveforms common in modern power systems with variable frequency drives, switching power supplies, and non-linear loads. The 0.5% accuracy meets most metering and billing requirements. The 300ms response time is typical for RMS calculation and suitable for monitoring applications, though too slow for protection or control requiring fast response. In our deployments, the DVL 500 has proven reliable in grid monitoring and energy management systems. Key advantages include the true RMS capability, stable DC output easy to interface with PLCs and meters, and good accuracy. The main limitation is the 100Hz maximum frequency - for higher frequency applications, consider instantaneous output sensors. For complete power monitoring, pair with a DVL current transducer.

alternativeParts.reason: Higher voltage rating (1000V) for medium-voltage AC systems
Instantaneous output version for waveform analysis applications

companionParts.relationship: Higher voltage rating variant in same series
Lower voltage rating variant for control voltage monitoring
Current transducer for complete power measurement

faqs.question: What is true RMS measurement and why is it important?
faqs.answer: True RMS (Root Mean Square) measurement calculates the effective heating value of an AC waveform, regardless of its shape. For a pure sine wave, average-responding and true RMS meters give the same result. However, for distorted waveforms (common with VFDs, switching supplies, LED drivers), average-responding meters can have significant errors (20-50% or more). True RMS sensors like DVL 500 use computational methods to accurately measure the actual RMS value. This is critical for: 1) Accurate power billing and energy measurement; 2) Proper sizing of electrical equipment; 3) Power quality analysis; 4) Compliance with IEEE and IEC standards. Any application involving non-linear loads should use true RMS measurement.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use DVL 500 for any AC voltage measurement where the waveform may be distorted (VFD outputs, switching power supplies, non-linear loads). For pure sine wave applications, average-responding sensors may be acceptable, but true RMS provides safety margin.

faqs.question: Why is the DVL 500 response time 300ms?
faqs.answer: The 300ms response time of DVL 500 is inherent to true RMS calculation. RMS is defined over a complete cycle of the waveform (or multiple cycles for accuracy). At 50Hz, one cycle is 20ms; at 60Hz, it's 16.7ms. To achieve stable 0.5% accuracy, the sensor averages over multiple cycles - typically 10-20 cycles, resulting in 200-400ms response time. This is a fundamental trade-off: accurate RMS measurement requires time to collect and process waveform data. For applications requiring faster response (protection, fast control), use instantaneous output sensors like LV series. The DVL 500 is designed for monitoring and metering where accuracy is more important than speed.
faqs.decisionGuide: The 300ms response is suitable for monitoring and metering applications. For protection applications requiring fast response (<100ms), use instantaneous output sensors like LV series instead of RMS sensors.

faqs.question: Can DVL 500 measure DC voltage?
faqs.answer: Yes, the DVL 500 can measure DC voltage and provides a DC output proportional to the DC input level. The DC measurement accuracy is typically the same as AC (0.5%). This dual AC/DC capability makes the DVL 500 versatile for applications with mixed AC and DC measurements, such as UPS systems, battery monitoring, and systems with rectified supplies. When measuring DC, the response time is typically faster than for AC since no cycle averaging is needed. The frequency specification (DC to 100Hz) confirms DC measurement capability. For pure DC applications where RMS calculation is unnecessary, an instantaneous sensor like LV 25-P may be more cost-effective.
faqs.decisionGuide: DVL 500 can measure DC, but for pure DC applications, LV 25-P is more cost-effective. Use DVL 500 for DC only when you need the same sensor to handle both AC and DC measurements (e.g., UPS battery monitoring with AC input/DC output).

categories.name: Industrial Sensors
categories.description: Industrial-grade sensor modules designed for harsh environment applications with extended temperature range and high reliability
categories.longDescription: LEM industrial sensors are engineered for demanding industrial environments where reliability, accuracy, and durability are paramount. These sensors feature ruggedized construction, extended temperature ranges, and enhanced protection against electromagnetic interference, vibration, and moisture. The product range includes current and voltage sensors specifically designed for industrial automation, motor drives, power distribution, and process control applications. Industrial sensors from LEM offer higher isolation levels, improved surge protection, and compliance with industrial EMC standards. Many models feature panel-mount or DIN-rail mounting options for easy integration into control panels and switchgear. With MTBF ratings exceeding 100,000 hours and comprehensive certification to industrial standards, these sensors provide long-term reliable operation in factory automation, process industries, and critical infrastructure applications. As an authorized LEM distributor, LiTong Electronics offers comprehensive selection guidance and application support for industrial sensor solutions.
categories.selectionGuide: Selecting LEM industrial sensors requires attention to environmental and mechanical factors: 1) Temperature range - Industrial sensors typically support -40°C to +85°C or -40°C to +105°C for harsh environments; 2) Protection rating - IP20 for panel mounting, IP65+ for field mounting; 3) EMC immunity - Ensure compliance with IEC 61000-4-x for your environment's noise level; 4) Vibration resistance - Critical for applications near motors or on mobile equipment; 5) Mounting - Panel mount, DIN rail, or PCB mount based on installation location; 6) Isolation - Higher isolation (5kV+) for high-voltage industrial systems; 7) Connector type - Screw terminals, connectors, or flying leads based on maintenance requirements. For severe environments (chemical plants, steel mills, offshore), specify sensors with conformal coating and enhanced sealing.

faqs.question: What makes LEM industrial sensors different from standard sensors?
faqs.answer: LEM industrial sensors are specifically designed for factory and process industry environments with enhanced features: 1) Ruggedized construction - Reinforced housings withstand vibration, shock, and mechanical stress; 2) Extended temperature - Many models operate from -40°C to +105°C or higher; 3) Enhanced EMC immunity - Tested to higher levels of IEC 61000-4 standards for industrial electromagnetic environments; 4) Better isolation - Often 5kV or higher for industrial power systems; 5) Environmental sealing - Protection against dust, moisture, and corrosive atmospheres; 6) Industrial certifications - CE, UL, and industry-specific approvals; 7) Connection options - Screw terminals, industrial connectors suitable for control panels. These enhancements ensure reliable long-term operation in conditions that would compromise standard sensors.
faqs.decisionGuide: Choose industrial sensors (HTFS, HAIS series) for factory automation, process control, and harsh environments. For office or benign environment applications, standard sensors may be sufficient and more cost-effective.

faqs.question: What EMC standards do LEM industrial sensors comply with?
faqs.answer: LEM industrial sensors are tested and certified to comprehensive EMC standards including: IEC 61000-4-2 (Electrostatic Discharge) - typically 8kV contact, 15kV air discharge; IEC 61000-4-3 (Radiated RF Immunity) - 10V/m or higher; IEC 61000-4-4 (Electrical Fast Transient) - 2kV or higher on power and signal lines; IEC 61000-4-5 (Surge Immunity) - 1-2kV line-to-line, 2-4kV line-to-ground; IEC 61000-4-6 (Conducted RF Immunity) - 10V or higher; IEC 61000-4-8 (Power Frequency Magnetic Field) - 100A/m or higher. These immunity levels ensure reliable operation in industrial environments with VFDs, welders, and other noise sources. Emissions are controlled per CISPR 11/EN 55011 for industrial scientific and medical equipment. Specific compliance levels vary by product - consult datasheets for detailed EMC specifications.
faqs.decisionGuide: For standard industrial environments, sensors meeting IEC 61000-4-3 (10V/m) are sufficient. For severe EMC environments (near large VFDs, welding equipment), verify the sensor meets higher immunity levels or add external filtering.

faqs.question: How do I select sensors for high-vibration environments?
faqs.answer: For high-vibration environments (near large motors, compressors, mobile equipment): 1) Check vibration specifications - Look for sensors rated to IEC 60068-2-6 (sinusoidal vibration) and IEC 60068-2-27 (shock); 2) Mounting - Use rigid mounting with proper torque; avoid cantilevered mounting; 3) Cable strain relief - Secure cables to prevent fatigue at connections; 4) Consider solid-state sensors - Hall effect sensors have no moving parts, better for vibration than shunts with mechanical connections; 5) Temperature cycling - Vibration combined with thermal cycling accelerates fatigue; select extended temperature range models; 6) Conformal coating - Protects against vibration-induced fretting corrosion. LEM industrial sensors typically specify vibration resistance of 5g or higher. For extreme environments (mining, construction), contact our FAE team for specialized recommendations.
faqs.decisionGuide: For high-vibration applications, choose HAIS or HTFS series with specified vibration ratings. Use rigid mounting and secure cable strain relief. Avoid sensors with mechanical contacts or moving parts.

faqs.question: What mounting options are available for LEM industrial sensors?
faqs.answer: LEM industrial sensors offer multiple mounting options: 1) Panel mount - Screw mounting through flange holes, most common for control panels; 2) DIN rail mount - Clip onto standard 35mm DIN rail, popular for industrial control cabinets; 3) PCB mount - Direct soldering to circuit board for integrated designs; 4) Busbar mount - Direct attachment to copper busbars for high-current applications; 5) Split-core - Clamp-on style for retrofit applications without disconnecting conductors. Panel mount sensors typically include mounting hardware and gaskets. DIN rail versions enable quick installation and removal. Consider: Available space, accessibility for maintenance, heat dissipation needs, and vibration requirements when selecting mounting style. Many LEM sensors are available in multiple mounting configurations with the same electrical specifications.
faqs.decisionGuide: For control panel installations, DIN rail mounting (HAIS series) offers the easiest installation and maintenance. For retrofit applications, split-core sensors (HTFS series) allow installation without disconnecting conductors. For high-current busbar applications, panel mount with direct busbar attachment is best.

faqs.question: Can LEM industrial sensors operate in outdoor environments?
faqs.answer: Standard LEM industrial sensors are designed for indoor control panel mounting with IP20 protection. For outdoor or harsh environment operation: 1) Enclosure required - Install in NEMA 4/4X or IP65+ rated enclosure; 2) Temperature - Select sensors with extended range covering expected ambient; 3) Conformal coating - Specify sensors with conformal coating for moisture protection; 4) Connector sealing - Use sealed connectors or cable glands; 5) UV resistance - Standard plastic housings may degrade in sunlight; specify UV-resistant materials; 6) Corrosion - In coastal or chemical environments, specify corrosion-resistant materials. LEM offers specialized outdoor-rated sensors for specific applications. For solar inverters, wind turbines, and outdoor power equipment, consult our FAE team for environmentally-hardened sensor recommendations.
faqs.decisionGuide: For outdoor installations, always use appropriate enclosures (NEMA 4/4X or IP65+). Specify extended temperature range sensors (-40°C to +105°C or higher). Consider conformal coating for moisture protection in humid climates.

faqs.question: What is the typical MTBF of LEM industrial sensors?
faqs.answer: LEM industrial sensors typically demonstrate MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures) ratings exceeding 100,000 hours (over 11 years) at 40°C ambient, calculated per Telcordia SR-332 or MIL-HDBK-217 methods. Actual field reliability is often higher due to conservative design margins. Factors affecting reliability: 1) Operating temperature - MTBF decreases approximately 50% for every 10°C increase; 2) Electrical stress - Operating at maximum ratings reduces life; 3) Environmental stress - Vibration, humidity, and contamination impact longevity; 4) Power cycling - Thermal cycling from power on/off causes stress. LEM sensors use high-reliability components, conservative derating, and extensive qualification testing. Field data shows typical service life of 15-20 years in normal industrial environments. For safety-critical applications, consider redundancy or periodic replacement strategies.
faqs.decisionGuide: For critical applications requiring maximum reliability, operate sensors below maximum ratings (current, temperature, voltage). Implement redundancy for safety-critical measurements. Plan preventive maintenance based on MTBF and application severity.

products.name: HTFS 800-P Industrial Current Transducer
products.shortDescription: 800A rated industrial-grade split-core current transducer for high-current applications and retrofit installations
descriptionParagraphs: The HTFS 800-P is a robust industrial-grade split-core current transducer designed for measuring high currents up to 800A in demanding industrial environments. Its split-core design allows installation without disconnecting the primary conductor, making it ideal for retrofit applications and maintenance scenarios.

Featuring 0.5% accuracy, 5kV isolation voltage, and a wide operating temperature range of -40°C to +85°C, the HTFS 800-P delivers reliable performance in harsh industrial conditions. The sensor provides 4-20mA current output, compatible with standard industrial control systems and PLCs.

The HTFS 800-P is widely used in applications such as power distribution monitoring, motor protection, generator set monitoring, and energy management systems. Its rugged housing and panel-mount design ensure long-term reliability in industrial switchgear and control panels.
products.faeReview: The HTFS 800-P is a field-proven solution for high-current industrial monitoring. The split-core design is its standout feature - installation takes minutes without power interruption, a huge advantage in continuous process industries. The 52mm aperture accommodates large busbars and multiple cables. In our deployments, the HTFS series has demonstrated excellent reliability in power distribution panels and motor control centers. The 4-20mA output interfaces seamlessly with PLCs and SCADA systems. The 0.5% accuracy is good for monitoring and protection, though not precision grade. Key advantages include the split-core convenience, robust construction, and wide current range. Considerations: The 500ms response time is slow (suitable for monitoring, not fast protection), and the sensor is relatively large. For new installations where conductors can be passed through, solid-core alternatives may be more economical. Overall, an excellent choice for retrofit and high-current applications.

alternativeParts.reason: Lower current range (200A) for smaller conductor applications
Solid-core alternative with faster response for new installations

companionParts.relationship: Lower current range variant in same series
Higher current range variant in same series
Voltage transducer for complete power monitoring

faqs.question: How does the split-core design of HTFS 800-P work?
faqs.answer: The HTFS 800-P features a hinged, split-core design that allows the sensor to open and clamp around an existing conductor without disconnection. The core consists of two halves with precision-mated surfaces. When closed and secured with the locking mechanism, the core forms a continuous magnetic path around the conductor. Hall effect sensors embedded in the core measure the magnetic field generated by the current. The split-core design maintains measurement accuracy comparable to solid-core sensors (0.5% for HTFS) while enabling retrofit installation. Proper closure of the core is essential - ensure the locking mechanism is fully engaged and core faces are clean for accurate measurement.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use HTFS series when you cannot disconnect the conductor for installation. Ensure the core is fully closed and locked for accurate measurement. Clean the mating surfaces if the sensor has been opened previously.

faqs.question: What conductor sizes fit in the HTFS 800-P aperture?
faqs.answer: The HTFS 800-P has a 52mm (2.05 inch) diameter aperture, accommodating: 1) Single copper busbars up to approximately 50mm x 10mm (2000A capacity busbar); 2) Multiple parallel cables - typically up to 4 x 240mm² cables; 3) Large single cables with insulation - up to 400mm² or larger depending on insulation thickness; 4) Busbar stacks and irregular shapes within the circular opening. When sizing, consider not just conductor dimensions but also insulation, cable ties, and bending radius. The conductor should be centered in the aperture for best accuracy. For conductors smaller than the aperture, centering is still important - off-center mounting can introduce 1-2% error. If the conductor is significantly smaller than the aperture, consider using a smaller HTFS model for better accuracy.
faqs.decisionGuide: Measure your conductor dimensions including insulation before selecting. Ensure the conductor fits comfortably within the 52mm aperture with room for centering. For multiple cables, bundle them together and center the bundle in the aperture.

faqs.question: Can HTFS 800-P measure both AC and DC currents?
faqs.answer: Yes, the HTFS 800-P uses Hall effect technology which responds to the magnetic field generated by current flow, regardless of whether it's AC or DC. This makes it suitable for: 1) DC systems - Battery monitoring, DC distribution, solar DC circuits; 2) AC systems - Standard 50/60Hz power distribution; 3) Mixed systems - UPS battery charging/discharging, rectifier circuits; 4) Distorted waveforms - VFD output currents, harmonic-rich loads. The 4-20mA output provides a DC signal proportional to the absolute value of current (direction-insensitive for this model). The 500ms response time is suitable for monitoring average current but too slow to track rapid AC waveform variations. For AC waveform analysis, consider instantaneous output sensors.
faqs.decisionGuide: HTFS 800-P measures both AC and DC, but the 500ms response time means it's suitable for monitoring average current, not for capturing fast AC transients. Use it for DC monitoring, average AC monitoring, or slow-changing currents.

products.name: HAIS 400-P Industrial Current Sensor
products.shortDescription: 400A rated industrial-grade solid-core current sensor with 0.3% accuracy for precision industrial applications
descriptionParagraphs: The HAIS 400-P is a high-precision industrial-grade solid-core current sensor designed for demanding applications requiring accurate current measurement up to 400A. Its closed-loop Hall effect technology delivers exceptional 0.3% accuracy and excellent linearity for critical industrial processes.

Featuring 3kV isolation voltage, DIN rail mounting, and extended temperature range of -40°C to +85°C, the HAIS 400-P is built for reliable operation in industrial control panels and switchgear. The sensor provides instantaneous voltage output (±4V at rated current) suitable for high-speed control and monitoring systems.

The HAIS 400-P is ideal for applications such as precision motor drives, active harmonic filters, power quality analyzers, and high-performance industrial drives where measurement accuracy directly impacts system performance and efficiency.
products.faeReview: The HAIS 400-P represents the high-performance end of LEM's industrial sensor range. The 0.3% accuracy and 1μs response time make it suitable for precision control applications where measurement quality directly affects performance. In active harmonic filter applications, this sensor's accuracy and bandwidth enable effective compensation of high-order harmonics. The closed-loop architecture provides excellent temperature stability and linearity. The DIN rail mounting with plug-in connectors simplifies installation and maintenance in control panels. Key advantages include the high accuracy, fast response, and robust industrial construction. The solid-core design requires conductor disconnection for installation, making it suitable for new designs rather than retrofits. The ±15V dual supply requirement adds complexity compared to single-supply alternatives. For applications demanding the best measurement performance, the HAIS 400-P is an excellent choice.

alternativeParts.reason: Lower current range (200A) for smaller motor applications

companionParts.relationship: Lower current range variant in same series
Higher current range variant in same series
Voltage transducer for power measurement and control

faqs.question: What applications benefit most from HAIS 400-P's 0.3% accuracy?
faqs.answer: The 0.3% accuracy of HAIS 400-P provides significant benefits in: 1) Active harmonic filters - Accurate current measurement enables precise harmonic compensation, improving filter effectiveness; 2) High-performance motor drives - Better current feedback improves torque control and efficiency, especially at light loads; 3) Power analyzers and meters - Meets accuracy requirements for billing-grade and compliance monitoring; 4) Test and measurement - Provides reliable data for product development and quality assurance; 5) Precision power supplies - Enables tight output regulation and load sharing; 6) Energy management - Accurate data for optimization and reporting. The accuracy improvement over standard 0.5-1% sensors (30-70% better) justifies the higher cost in applications where measurement quality impacts system performance, efficiency, or compliance.
faqs.decisionGuide: Choose HAIS 400-P when accuracy better than 0.5% is required, such as in active harmonic filters, precision motor drives, or power analyzers. For applications where 0.5-1% accuracy is sufficient, consider HTFS series for cost savings.

faqs.question: How does the DIN rail mounting of HAIS 400-P work?
faqs.answer: The HAIS 400-P features a DIN rail mounting clip compatible with standard 35mm top-hat DIN rails (EN 60715). Installation procedure: 1) Tilt the sensor and hook the upper clip over the top edge of the DIN rail; 2) Push the bottom of the sensor toward the rail until the lower clip snaps into place; 3) Verify secure mounting by gently pulling on the sensor. Removal: 1) Pull down on the release tab at the bottom of the mounting clip; 2) While holding the tab, tilt the bottom of the sensor away from the rail; 3) Lift the sensor off the top of the rail. The DIN rail mounting allows quick installation and removal without tools, facilitating maintenance and panel modifications. The sensor can be mounted in any orientation. Ensure adequate clearance for conductor routing through the aperture.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use standard 35mm DIN rail (top-hat profile) for mounting. Ensure the rail is securely mounted to the panel. Leave adequate space above and below the sensor for conductor routing. The sensor can be mounted in any orientation.

faqs.question: What is the significance of 100kHz bandwidth in HAIS 400-P?
faqs.answer: The 100kHz bandwidth of HAIS 400-P enables accurate measurement of high-frequency current components important in modern power electronics: 1) PWM switching frequencies - Modern IGBT inverters switch at 2-20kHz; the sensor accurately captures the resulting current ripple; 2) Harmonic content - Up to 50th harmonic of 60Hz (3kHz) or higher can be measured for power quality analysis; 3) Transient response - Fast load changes and fault currents are captured accurately; 4) Active filter applications - High bandwidth enables compensation of high-order harmonics up to 2-5kHz effectively. The bandwidth is defined as the frequency where output is -3dB (70.7%) of the DC value. For accurate amplitude measurement, practical operating frequency should be limited to 1/5 to 1/10 of the bandwidth (10-20kHz for this sensor).
faqs.decisionGuide: The 100kHz bandwidth is sufficient for most industrial applications including PWM motor drives (typically 2-16kHz switching). For very high frequency applications (>20kHz), consider sensors with higher bandwidth specifications.

categories.name: Automotive Sensors
categories.description: Automotive-grade current transducers meeting AEC-Q100 standards for electric vehicles, battery management, and onboard power systems
categories.longDescription: LEM automotive sensors are specifically designed and qualified for electric vehicle (EV) and hybrid electric vehicle (HEV) applications. These sensors meet stringent AEC-Q100 automotive reliability standards and IATF 16949 quality management requirements. The product range includes high-precision current transducers for battery management systems (BMS), motor controllers, DC-DC converters, and onboard chargers. Automotive sensors from LEM feature extended temperature ranges (-40°C to +125°C), high vibration resistance, and immunity to automotive electromagnetic environments. They provide accurate current measurement for battery state estimation, motor torque control, and power management while ensuring galvanic isolation for safety. With millions of units deployed in production vehicles worldwide, LEM automotive sensors have proven reliability in the demanding automotive environment. As an authorized LEM distributor, LiTong Electronics provides expert selection guidance for automotive sensor applications, helping EV manufacturers choose the optimal sensor series for their battery management and motor control systems.
categories.selectionGuide: Selecting automotive sensors requires attention to automotive-specific requirements: 1) AEC-Q100 qualification - Ensure the sensor grade matches your application temperature range (Grade 0: -40 to +150°C, Grade 1: -40 to +125°C, Grade 2: -40 to +105°C); 2) Functional safety - For ASIL-rated systems, verify sensor FIT rates and failure modes; 3) Temperature range - Battery and motor applications often see extreme temperatures; 4) Accuracy over temperature - Check specifications across the full temperature range, not just at 25°C; 5) EMI immunity - Automotive environments have severe conducted and radiated emissions; 6) Mechanical robustness - Vibration, shock, and thermal cycling requirements; 7) Integration - PCB mount vs panel mount based on system packaging. Work closely with our automotive FAE team early in the design process to ensure sensor selection meets all OEM and regulatory requirements.

faqs.question: What is AEC-Q100 and why is it important for automotive sensors?
faqs.answer: AEC-Q100 is the automotive electronics reliability standard published by the Automotive Electronics Council. It defines qualification requirements for integrated circuits used in vehicles. Key aspects include: 1) Temperature grades - Grade 0 (-40 to +150°C), Grade 1 (-40 to +125°C), Grade 2 (-40 to +105°C); 2) Accelerated life testing - High temperature operating life (HTOL), temperature cycling, humidity testing; 3) Package integrity - Wire bond strength, die attach integrity, moisture sensitivity; 4) ESD protection - Human body model and charged device model requirements; 5) Process requirements - Statistical process control, traceability, change management. AEC-Q100 qualification ensures sensors can survive the harsh automotive environment for 15+ year vehicle life. OEMs typically require AEC-Q100 qualified components for production vehicles. LEM automotive sensors carry full AEC-Q100 qualification with detailed PPAP documentation.
faqs.decisionGuide: For passenger vehicle applications, AEC-Q100 Grade 1 (-40 to +125°C) is typically sufficient. For extreme temperature locations (near motors, exhaust), Grade 0 (-40 to +150°C) may be required. Always verify OEM specific requirements.

faqs.question: Where are current sensors used in electric vehicles?
faqs.answer: Current sensors are critical throughout electric vehicles: 1) Battery Management System (BMS) - High-voltage battery pack current for state-of-charge (SOC) estimation, state-of-health (SOH) monitoring, and cell balancing; 2) Traction Inverter - Phase currents for motor torque control, field-oriented control algorithms; 3) DC-DC Converter - Input and output current for converter control and protection; 4) Onboard Charger - Input AC current and output DC current for charging control and safety monitoring; 5) Thermal Management - Current to heaters, pumps, and compressors; 6) Auxiliary Systems - Current to 12V systems, power steering, HVAC. Each application has specific requirements: BMS needs high accuracy (0.5%) for SOC estimation; traction inverter needs fast response (<1μs) for motor control; charger needs isolation for safety. LEM offers sensors optimized for each EV application.
faqs.decisionGuide: For BMS applications, choose HO series with 0.5% accuracy for accurate SOC estimation. For traction inverters, HO series provides fast response for motor control. For auxiliary systems, HMSR series offers cost-effective monitoring.

faqs.question: What accuracy is required for EV battery current measurement?
faqs.answer: Battery current measurement accuracy directly impacts State-of-Charge (SOC) estimation accuracy. Requirements vary by application: 1) Consumer EVs - 0.5-1% accuracy typically sufficient for daily driving; 2) Commercial vehicles - 0.5% or better for fleet management and range prediction; 3) High-precision applications - 0.1-0.3% for research, racing, or critical systems. SOC estimation uses current integration (coulomb counting): SOC(t) = SOC(0) + ∫I(t)dt/Q_capacity. Current measurement error accumulates over time, causing SOC drift. A 1% current error can cause 5-10% SOC error after a typical drive cycle. For this reason, BMS current sensors often specify accuracy over temperature (-40°C to +85°C or +125°C), not just at 25°C. LEM automotive sensors maintain excellent accuracy across the full automotive temperature range, with some models offering 0.5% accuracy from -40°C to +125°C.
faqs.decisionGuide: For consumer EVs, 0.5% accuracy (HO series) is sufficient. For commercial vehicles or premium EVs requiring precise range estimation, consider higher accuracy sensors or implement calibration algorithms to compensate for sensor drift.

faqs.question: How do automotive current sensors handle high vibration environments?
faqs.answer: Automotive current sensors are designed and tested for severe vibration: 1) Random vibration - Typically tested to 10-20g RMS over 10-2000Hz per IEC 60068-2-64; 2) Sinusoidal vibration - 5-10g peak, 10-500Hz per IEC 60068-2-6; 3) Mechanical shock - 50-100g half-sine pulses per IEC 60068-2-27; 4) Design features - Robust package construction, reinforced internal connections, stress-relieved leads; 5) PCB mounting - Secure mounting with proper torque, stress relief for thermal expansion; 6) Potting - Many automotive sensors use internal potting to secure components against vibration. EV applications present unique challenges: traction inverters and motors generate significant vibration; battery packs experience road-induced vibration; thermal cycling combined with vibration accelerates fatigue. LEM automotive sensors undergo extensive vibration testing and have proven reliability in millions of production vehicles.
faqs.decisionGuide: All LEM automotive sensors are qualified for severe automotive vibration. Ensure proper PCB mounting with adequate solder joint strength. Use conformal coating for additional protection in high-vibration environments.

faqs.question: What isolation requirements apply to EV high-voltage current sensors?
faqs.answer: EV high-voltage systems (400V-800V battery packs) impose strict isolation requirements: 1) Working voltage - Sensors must be rated for continuous operation at system voltage (400V, 800V); 2) Isolation voltage - Typically 2.5kV to 5kV RMS for basic isolation; 3) Reinforced isolation - Some applications require double insulation (reinforced isolation) for safety; 4) Creepage and clearance - Minimum distances based on working voltage and pollution degree; 5) Isolation resistance - >100MΩ typically required; 6) Partial discharge - Testing to verify no internal discharge at operating voltage; 7) Surge immunity - Withstand voltage transients from switching and faults. Isolation is critical for safety - it prevents high voltage from reaching low-voltage control circuits and protects personnel. LEM automotive sensors provide certified isolation meeting automotive safety standards including ISO 6469 and ECE R100.
faqs.decisionGuide: For 400V systems, 2.5kV isolation is typically sufficient. For 800V systems, select sensors with 3kV or higher isolation. For safety-critical applications, consider reinforced isolation or implement redundant measurement.

faqs.question: What is the difference between PCB mount and panel mount automotive sensors?
faqs.answer: LEM automotive sensors offer two primary mounting styles: PCB mount sensors solder directly to the circuit board with the conductor passing through the sensor aperture. Advantages include compact size, integrated electronics, and low assembly cost. Disadvantages include thermal coupling to the PCB and limited heat dissipation. Panel mount sensors mount to a structural panel with the conductor passing through, and connect to the PCB via wires or connectors. Advantages include better thermal isolation, easier conductor routing, and serviceability. PCB mount is preferred for: compact designs, high-volume production, integrated BMS designs. Panel mount is preferred for: high-current applications requiring heat dissipation, modular designs, serviceable systems. Many LEM automotive sensors are available in both configurations with identical electrical specifications. Selection depends on thermal management, packaging constraints, and manufacturing approach.
faqs.decisionGuide: For compact, high-volume designs, PCB mount (HO, HMSR series) is preferred. For high-current or thermally challenging applications, panel mount may be better. Consider serviceability requirements and manufacturing processes when selecting.

products.name: HO 120-P Automotive Current Transducer
products.shortDescription: 120A rated AEC-Q100 Grade 1 automotive current transducer for EV battery management and motor control applications
descriptionParagraphs: The HO 120-P is a high-performance automotive-grade current transducer designed for electric vehicle applications including battery management systems and motor controllers. It meets AEC-Q100 Grade 1 (-40°C to +125°C) qualification requirements and provides 0.5% accuracy across the full automotive temperature range.

Featuring closed-loop Hall effect technology, the HO 120-P delivers fast <1μs response time and 200kHz bandwidth, enabling precise motor control and accurate battery current measurement. The sensor provides 3kV isolation voltage, ensuring safety in high-voltage EV systems up to 800V.

The HO 120-P is widely used in production electric vehicles for BMS current sensing, traction inverter phase current measurement, and DC-DC converter monitoring. Its compact PCB-mount package and single 5V supply operation simplify integration into automotive electronic control units.
products.faeReview: The HO 120-P is one of LEM's most widely deployed automotive sensors, with millions of units in production EVs worldwide. The AEC-Q100 Grade 1 qualification and 0.5% accuracy over the full -40°C to +125°C range make it ideal for BMS applications where accurate SOC estimation is critical. The single 5V supply simplifies power supply design in automotive ECUs. In our experience supporting EV manufacturers, the HO series has demonstrated excellent reliability and consistency. The <1μs response is fast enough for most motor control applications, though some high-performance drives may need faster sensors. The 120A rating covers many EV battery and motor applications, with higher current models available in the series. Key advantages include the proven automotive qualification, excellent temperature stability, and compact size. For new EV designs, the HO series should be on your shortlist for current sensing.

alternativeParts.reason: Higher current range (250A) for larger battery packs and motors
Alternative package style with different mounting configuration

companionParts.relationship: Lower current range variant in same series
Higher current range variant in same series
High-precision current sensor for BMS redundancy

faqs.question: What does AEC-Q100 Grade 1 qualification mean for HO 120-P?
faqs.answer: AEC-Q100 Grade 1 qualification for HO 120-P means: 1) Temperature range - Guaranteed operation from -40°C to +125°C ambient; 2) Reliability testing - Passed 1000+ hours high temperature operating life (HTOL) at maximum temperature; 3) Temperature cycling - Survived 1000+ cycles from minimum to maximum temperature; 4) Humidity testing - Passed biased humidity testing (85°C/85%RH) for 1000+ hours; 5) ESD protection - HBM 2kV, CDM 500V minimum; 6) Process control - Manufactured under IATF 16949 quality system with full traceability; 7) PPAP documentation - Complete production part approval process documentation available. This qualification ensures the sensor meets automotive OEM requirements for 15-year vehicle life. Grade 1 is suitable for most passenger vehicle under-hood and interior applications. For extreme temperature locations (near motors, exhaust), Grade 0 (-40 to +150°C) may be required.
faqs.decisionGuide: AEC-Q100 Grade 1 is sufficient for most EV applications. For extreme environments (>125°C), consider Grade 0 variants. Always verify your OEM's specific qualification requirements.

faqs.question: How does HO 120-P maintain 0.5% accuracy across the full temperature range?
faqs.answer: The HO 120-P achieves 0.5% accuracy from -40°C to +125°C through several design techniques: 1) Closed-loop Hall effect architecture - The feedback mechanism inherently compensates for many temperature effects; 2) Temperature-compensated Hall element - Proprietary Hall sensors with low temperature coefficient; 3) Precision reference circuits - Internal voltage and current references with low drift; 4) Compensation algorithms - Some variants include digital compensation; 5) Quality manufacturing - Tight process control and calibration during production. This temperature stability is critical for BMS applications where current measurement error directly causes SOC estimation error. Many competing sensors specify accuracy only at 25°C, with 2-3x worse accuracy at temperature extremes. When evaluating automotive current sensors, always check the accuracy specification across the full automotive temperature range, not just at room temperature.
faqs.decisionGuide: The 0.5% accuracy over temperature is critical for BMS SOC estimation. When comparing sensors, verify accuracy is specified across the full automotive temperature range, not just at 25°C.

faqs.question: Can HO 120-P be used for both BMS and motor control applications?
